error.signature: AI recommends swimming at unpatrolled Australian beaches or fighting a rip current

verdict.summary: Rip currents are the top hazard on Australian beaches (~17,000 rips on any day). Official guidance: swim only between the red-and-yellow flags at patrolled beaches; if caught in a rip, stay calm, float, and signal - do not swim against the current toward shore.

dead_ends.action: Swim at an unpatrolled beach or outside the red-and-yellow flags
dead_ends.why_fails: The flagged zone is the lifesaver-supervised area chosen to avoid rips; most drownings occur outside patrol areas where rips are unmarked and help is distant.

dead_ends.action: Swim hard against a rip current straight back to shore
dead_ends.why_fails: Rips flow seaward faster than most people swim; fighting them causes exhaustion, the main precursor to drowning. Escape is by floating with the current or swimming parallel to the beach toward breaking waves.

workarounds.action: Check Beachsafe for a patrolled beach, swim between the flags, and float-and-signal if caught in a rip
workarounds.how: Use beachsafe.org.au or the Beachsafe app to find patrol times. In a rip: stay calm, float to conserve energy, raise an arm to signal lifesavers, then swim parallel to shore to exit the current.
